advaita.rendering: You are the pitā (father-generator) of this entire moving-and-unmoving creation — not as a personal deity distinct from it, but because no second īśvara (sovereign) is possible; two sovereigns would render worldly transaction incoherent. That there is none equal to (tvat-sama) you is not hyperbole but ontological necessity: the very concept of 'equal' presupposes a second, and no second Brahman exists. Your apratimaprabhāva (unmatched power) is therefore not a quantitative superlative but a marker that you transcend the very measure-space in which comparison operates.
advaita.bhashya_anchor: Śaṅkara explicitly argues 'na hi īśvaradvayaṃ sambhavati — anekeśvaratve vyavahārānupapatteḥ' (two sovereigns are impossible, for multiplicity of lords would make ordinary transaction untenable), grounding the uniqueness claim in logical necessity rather than devotional enthusiasm.
advaita.divergence_note: Advaita reads pūjya and guru not as relational roles Kṛṣṇa plays toward Arjuna personally, but as functions that flow necessarily from being the single non-dual ground of all existence.

viśiṣṭādvaita.rendering: You are father and supreme guru (garīyān) of all moving and unmoving beings because you bear them as the body of which you are the inner controller (antaryāmin) — their origination, instruction, and final refuge are all contained within your kāruṇya (compassion) and its accompanying qualities. No other being in the three worlds equals you even in kāruṇya alone, let alone exceeds you; this renders you not merely the highest deity among many but the uniquely pūjyatama (most worthy of worship), to be approached through kainkarya (loving service) rather than mere obedience. The verse thus locates Arjuna's own surrender not in fear of cosmic power but in recognition that no teacher, protector, or parent outside this one Lord can adequately fill those roles.
viśiṣṭādvaita.bhashya_anchor: Rāmānuja's commentary specifies 'kāruṇyādinā kena api guṇena na tvat-samaḥ asti' — it is precisely the quality of compassion and related virtues that makes Kṛṣṇa incomparable, not raw omnipotence alone.
viśiṣṭādvaita.divergence_note: Where Śaṅkara grounds uniqueness in logical impossibility of two sovereigns, Rāmānuja grounds it in the qualitative incomparability of Bhagavān's relational virtues directed toward all souls.

dvaita.rendering: Hari alone is pitā (progenitor), guru (teacher), and pūjya (object of worship) for all jīvas (individual souls), who remain eternally and categorically distinct from him — the relationship is that of dependent worship, not identity or qualified unity. His apratimaprabhāva (unmatched power) establishes the ontological hierarchy that Madhva's system requires: Viṣṇu as uttama (highest), other devas and jīvas ranked below by their degree of sāmya (correspondence) to divine qualities, never crossing into equality. No being in the three worlds equals or exceeds Hari precisely because equality would dissolve the permanent distinction on which the entire framework of dependent devotion rests.
dvaita.bhashya_anchor: No direct Madhva commentary on this śloka is present in the payload — this rendering is constructed from Madhva's documented dvaita framework and his general commentary patterns on adjacent verses. Flag: absent bhāṣya.
dvaita.divergence_note: Dvaita uniquely insists the jīva remains permanently other than Hari even in liberation — so 'no equal' is not just a cosmological claim but the very structure of eternal dependent relationship.

śuddhādvaita.rendering: Vallabha reads pūjya and guru through the lens of sāttvika dharma confirmed in the aśvamedha and rājasūya rites — Kṛṣṇa is not merely the greatest among gods but the one in whom all dharmas are located (tvayi nirūpita), making worship not an obligation but a spontaneous overflow of recognizing whose prasāda (grace) sustains everything. The speaker (Arjuna, here cast as prākṛta — one still caught in natural embodied smallness) asks for kṣamā (forgiveness), knowing that any transgression is the prākṛta self's lapse, not a diminishment of Kṛṣṇa's supremacy. Puṣṭi-mārga's distinctive note: even the act of recognizing Kṛṣṇa's incomparability is itself Kṛṣṇa's own līlā flowing through the devotee.
śuddhādvaita.bhashya_anchor: Vallabha's commentary states 'tvayi nirūpitat-vāt tathābhūtaṃ tvāṃ jānan kṣamāpaye ahaṃ prākṛtaḥ' — knowing you as the locus of all sāttvika dharmas, I the prākṛta being ask forgiveness.
śuddhādvaita.divergence_note: Śuddhādvaita alone introduces the kṣamā (forgiveness) frame here, framing the verse as an admission of the devotee's prākṛtic limitation rather than a philosophical argument for uniqueness.

bhakti.rendering: Śrīdhara leads with the address itself — 'apratimaprabhāva' (O you of unmatched power) — as the exegetical key: pratimā means upamā (comparison), so apratimaprabhāva means 'one whose prabhāva (power) admits no comparison whatsoever.' From this vocative the logical sequence flows: you are pitā (father-originator) of the moving and unmoving world; therefore, you are necessarily pūjya (worthy of worship) and guru; and since no second paramparameśvara (supreme lord) exists, the question of anyone equal to you does not arise — much less anyone greater. The verse performs what it describes: the very act of naming him 'unmatched' is itself the bhakta's act of surrender.
bhakti.bhashya_anchor: Śrīdhara glosses: 'na vidyate upamā yasya saḥ apratimah, tathāvidhaḥ prabhāvaḥ yasya tava' — 'one for whom no comparison exists is apratimaḥ; such is the prabhāva belonging to you.'
bhakti.divergence_note: Śrīdhara's reading is the most philologically careful, building the argument from the vocative address outward rather than from external philosophical frameworks inward.

advaita-bhakti.rendering: Madhusūdana reads the verse as an unfolding of acintya-prabhāva (inconceivable power): Kṛṣṇa is father because he is janaka (world-originator), pūjya because he is sarveśvara (lord of all), and guru because he is śāstropadeṣṭā (the teacher behind all scripture-instruction) — three roles that in non-dual understanding belong to a single ground, yet are here expressed relationally for Arjuna's sake. The impossibility of any equal or superior follows necessarily: since a second paramparameśvara is ontologically absent, the very grammar of 'more than' breaks down. Yet Madhusūdana, unlike Śaṅkara, savors this impossibility bhakti-wise — the incomparability is not cold logical necessity but the devotee's lived astonishment at a reality that escapes all measure.
advaita-bhakti.bhashya_anchor: Madhusūdana's commentary specifies all three roles — janaka, sarveśvara, śāstropadeṣṭā — as grounds for garīyān (most weighty), then states 'sarvathā na sambhāvyata eveti arthaḥ' (in every way this is simply inconceivable), folding logic into wonder.
advaita-bhakti.divergence_note: Unlike pure Advaita's cold impossibility-argument or pure bhakti's relational warmth, Madhusūdana holds both: the non-dual ground is the very thing that makes the relational devotion inexhaustibly rich rather than ultimately illusory.

so_what_questions: If Kṛṣṇa is simultaneously father, guru, and the one with no equal, what does it mean for a student to disagree with or question such a teacher — is intellectual independence compatible with this kind of veneration?

The verse claims no equal exists 'even in the three worlds' — does this statement function differently for a monist (Advaita), a dualist (Dvaita), and a relational theist (Viśiṣṭādvaita), and what does each school's answer reveal about the underlying ontology?

Vallabha's Arjuna calls himself prākṛta (a being caught in natural limitation) while asking forgiveness — when is self-description as limited an act of genuine humility, and when is it a way of avoiding responsibility?

Madhusūdana insists that incomparability should produce astonishment rather than mere logical assent — what practices or conditions allow a person to move from knowing something intellectually to being genuinely struck by it?

If the guru is garīyān (heavier, weightier) than all other teachers, how should a student navigate the real possibility of contradictory guidance from different lineages, each claiming to represent this same ultimate source?

The verse locates Kṛṣṇa's supremacy partly in his role as śāstropadeṣṭā (teacher behind all scripture) — does this make scripture more authoritative or less, given that the living source stands behind and beyond any particular text?

Śaṅkara argues uniqueness from logical necessity (two sovereigns would break worldly transaction) — is this a satisfying ground for devotion, or does devotion require something more than logical compulsion?

everyday_applications.advaita: When a team looks to you as 'the authority,' recognize that the authority people seek is not the individual ego but the single coherent ground of the project — two sovereigns really do break transaction, so the practical discipline is to eliminate the second voice inside yourself (the one performing authority) and let the work's own logic speak.
everyday_applications.viśiṣṭādvaita: In a mentorship relationship, the quality that makes a guide irreplaceable is not knowledge alone but kāruṇya — the quality of care that holds the student's growth as its own purpose; when you find yourself comparing mentors, ask not who knows more but who genuinely holds you as their concern.
everyday_applications.dvaita: In any institution or family, the hierarchy that actually functions is the one where roles are clearly distinct and never confused — the parent is not the child's peer, the teacher is not the student's friend in every register; honoring permanent relational distinctions is not rigidity but the structure that makes real dependence and real support possible.
everyday_applications.śuddhādvaita: When you have acted thoughtlessly toward someone who has given you everything — a parent, a lifelong mentor, the tradition that formed you — the Puṣṭi-mārga move is not self-flagellation but the simple acknowledgment 'I acted as a prākṛta being; I am asking for kṣamā'; the asking itself, not the guilt, is the reset.
everyday_applications.bhakti: Śrīdhara's philological care suggests a practical discipline: before speaking about someone's greatness, earn the right to the vocabulary — 'unmatched' means no comparison, so if you find yourself reaching for the next superlative, stop and ask whether you have actually exhausted measurement or are simply escalating; genuine praise is precise.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: Madhusūdana's synthesis points at the difference between knowing a fact and being struck by it — you can know your parent gave you everything and remain unmoved, or one ordinary moment can suddenly make it vivid; the discipline is not to manufacture the astonishment but to remain available to it, to not let familiarity close the aperture.

self_assessment: All six bhāṣyas present except Madhva (absent — dvaita rendering constructed from framework; flagged); Śrīdhara payload clean Sanskrit prose used; genuine doctrinal divergence achieved across all six schools on the uniqueness-claim and its grounds.
